cells.speakerNotes:
- Pre-print released March 2026
- Co-authored by Yann LeCun, one of the founding figures of modern deep learning
- 2018 Turing Award winner (shared with Bengio & Hinton) for contributions to deep learning
- Critic of autoregressive LLMs and model-free RL as paths to true intelligence
- In 2022: 60-page position paper outlining his vision for autonomous machine intelligence
- Central idea: the Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) — predict in latent space, not pixel space
- LeWorldModel: First JEPA-based world model to train end-to-end from raw pixels using only a two-terms loss

- Agenda for the next 20 minutes
  - First we'll cover the general JEPA paradigm
  - We'll identify representation collapse as its central challenge, then see how existing methods address it
  - We'll then walk through LeWorldModel's general architecture
  - How it handles collapse with a single hyperparameter
  - How this latent model is used for planning at inference time
  - We'll look at one experiment that I think best illustrates what the model can and can't do
  - We close with a short discussion: main results, limitations, and open questions

- Model takes raw pixels as input -> No preprocessing, no pre-trained features
  - Left encoder: sequence of video frames -> Temporal context observed so far
  - Right encoder: goal state image -> The target state the agent should reach
  - Both encoders map into the same latent space -> The "joint embedding" in the name; both trained end-to-end together
  - Predictor takes the context embedding + a latent variable -> Predicts what the goal embedding should look like
  - Latent variable captures uncertainty: Which of many plausible future states will actually occur
  - Loss: minimize distance between predicted and actual goal embedding -> Purely in latent space
  - No pixel decoder, no reconstruction — the model never needs to render what the goal looks like

- In the last slide, we saw that a JEPA trains by minimizing the distance between the predicted and the goal embedding
- Questions: Can you think of a way to achieve (near)-perfect loss without the model ever learning anything meaningful about the environment?
- No answer: Think about it from the optimizer's perspective. What's the path of least resistance when both the predicted and goal embeddings come from learned encoders
- What if both encoders output the exact same vector for every single input? Regardless of what the video or goal state actually looks like?

- Answer: The encoders can output the same vector for every input -> Two ways this can happen
- Complete collapse: Every input maps to the exact same point in the embedding space -> Predicted and goal embedding are always identical -> Zero loss, but the model has learned nothing
- Dimensional collapse: Embeddings don't collapse into a single point but only vary along a few dimensions
- Example: 512-dimensional embedding space where the model only uses 3 dimensions -> Result: The loss looks healthy, but the representation is almost useless for planning
- Central questions for JEPA-based methods are: How to prevent the representation collapse?

- Generative reconstruction (DreamerV4): predict pixels instead of latent vectors 
  - Collapse is impossible because the target is fixed in pixel space
  - Limitation: for planning and control, capacity spent on pixel-level detail (textures, lighting, background) doesn't contribute to better decisions 
  - A latent model can be more efficient for that specific use case
- EMA + stop-gradient (V-JEPA 2): two encoders 
  - An online encoder trained by gradient descent, and a target encoder updated only as a slow-moving average of the online encoder's weights
  - Stop-gradient means no gradients flow through the target encoder
  - The asymmetry is what prevents collapse
  - Limitation: there's no loss term that explicitly prevents collapse —> It's the asymmetry that happens to work empirically -> No theoretical guarantee.
- Frozen pretrained encoder (DINO-WM): fix the encoder entirely so it physically cannot collapse
  - Limitation: bounded by what the pretrained model learned -> The encoder never adapts to the task, and you lose end-to-end training
 - Explicit regularization (VICReg / PLDM): directly penalizes variance and covariance of embeddings -> Provably prevents collapse
   - Limitation: up to 6 loss hyperparameters to tune, and known training instabilities in practice

- Self-supervised representation learning (I-JEPA, V-JEPA 2): Predict masked latent patches 
  - Purely about learning good representations, no actions, no planning component at all
  - These are the models closest to the original JEPA vision
  - They're not world models in the RL sense, the agent can't use them to plan
- Generative world models (DreamerV4, Genie): action-conditioned simulators that operate in pixel space
  - The agent imagines future frames and plans through them
  - These can plan, but they do it by hallucinating pixels -> Computationally expensive and capacity is spent on visual fidelity rather than decision-relevant structure
- Latent action-conditioned world models (DINO-WM, PLDM): predict dynamics directly in latent space and plan by imagination there 
  - No pixel reconstruction at any point
  - This is the smallest family, and the one LeWM belongs to

- V-JEPA 2: End-to-end, but not built for control
- DreamerV4: End-to-end and does control, but requires reward and operates in pixel space
- DINO-WM: reward-free latent control, but the frozen encoder means it's not end-to-end 
  - It can only be as good as DINOv2
- PLDM: the closest predecessor: End-to-end, reward-free, latent control, 
  - Explicit regularization, but 6 hyperparameters -> Training instabilities
- LeWM is essentially PLDM with a better regularizer, same target task, same end-to-end training, but SIGReg replaces VICReg's 6 hyperparameters with 1

Task-T: Move a T-shaped object with an EEF to a goal position
  - Action a(t) is the EEF goal position (x, y) -> Position-controlled, the controller moves the end-effector there
  - Dynamics are non-trivial: contact between EEF and T causes irregular rotations/translations depending on where contact is made

  ViT-Tiny encoder
  - Receives a window of the last 3 observations: x(t-2), x(t-1), x(t) -> All encoded independently by the same ViT-Tiny (~5M params)
  - Multiple frames provide temporal context: the model can infer velocity and motion direction, not just a static snapshot

  Action conditioning via AdaLN
  - Action is not concatenated as a token but injected via AdaLN at every transformer layer
  - AdaLN projects the action into per-layer scale (γ) and shift (β), modulating the layer norm activations
  - Effect: the action steers the entire computation — every layer "knows" the goal, not just the input

  Loss
  - Prediction target: latent of the actual next frame x(t+1) encoded by the second ViT-Tiny
  - MSE: predicted latent must match the target latent
  - SIGReg: prevents representation collapse — without it, MSE alone is minimized trivially by mapping everything to a constant

Patch Tokenization
- The input image is split into a grid of fixed-size patches 
  - 224×224 image yields 16×16 = 256 patches of 14×14 pixels each
  - Each patch is flattened and linearly projected to a D=192-dimensional embedding 
  - This is the sole inductive bias injected: no convolution, no locality assumption
  - ViT has far less image-specific inductive bias than CNNs; spatial relations between patches must be learned from data, not baked in (Dosovitskiy et al., §3.1 "Inductive bias")

[CLS] Token
- A learnable classification token is prepended to the sequence (borrowed directly from BERT)
- Its state at the output of the final layer serves as the global image representation
  - It aggregates information from all patches via self-attention
  - Learnable 1D positional embeddings are added to all tokens

Transformer Encoder (ViT-Tiny)
 - 12 layers of alternating Multi-Head Self-Attention (MSA) and MLP blocks, with AdaptiveLayerNorm before each block and residual connections after 
 - ViT-Tiny: d=192, 3 attention heads, ~5M parameters 

LeWM-Specific Projection
- The [CLS] token passes through a 1-layer MLP + BatchNorm
- This projection is necessary: 
  - The final ViT layer applies LayerNorm, which normalizes the embeddings and prevents the SIGReg anti-collapse regularizer from being optimized effectively
  - BatchNorm restores the free statistics SIGReg needs

Why Layer Normalization?
  - Transformer activations can grow or shrink arbitrarily across many layers — LN re-centers each token's features to zero mean and unit variance, keeping gradients well-conditioned throughout
  - Unlike Batch Norm, LN normalizes across the feature dimension per token independently — no dependence on batch size, works for variable-length vision sequences
  - Placed before each sublayer (pre-LN), it stabilizes the residual stream so sublayers only need to learn small corrections on a clean, normalized input

  Standard LN
  - γ and β are fixed parameters learned once during training — identical rescaling for every input, regardless of what action was taken
  - No mechanism to inject external context: the model cannot adapt its internal representations based on the action

  AdaLN-Zero
  - Replaces fixed γ, β with three condition-dependent signals predicted from the action embedding c: scale Σ(c), shift Δ(c), and residual gate G(c)
  - The (1+Σ) form is deliberate: at Σ=0 the scale is 1, not 0 — avoids dead neurons at initialization
  - One SiLU linear projection per block outputs all 6 values — separate (Δ, Σ, G) for both the attention and MLP sublayers; no own LayerNorm affine parameters are needed
  - Both LayerNorms use elementwise_affine=False — full affine control is delegated to the action conditioning

Why scale + shift over concatenation or addition?
  - Concatenating an action token into the patch sequence: the action embedding is tiny relative to 256 patch tokens — its signal gets diluted in self-attention
  - Simple addition: gives only a uniform additive offset to all features — cannot modulate how strongly individual features respond to the action
  - AdaLN conditions both the scale and shift of every feature on the action, giving strictly more expressive per-feature control

  Why zero-init?
  - G=0 does not zero the signal — the gate only controls the sublayer's residual contribution: x ← x + G·sublayer(y), so G=0 gives x ← x, a pure skip connection; the latent passes through unchanged
  - At initialization the entire predictor transformer is an identity function — this is intentional: the encoder already produces meaningful latents from step 0, so no distortion is introduced while training stabilizes
  - Two-phase dynamic: SIGReg first anchors the latent geometry while the predictor is identity; then as training progresses, gradients teach the gates to open and the predictor learns to transform latents in a meaningful
  action-conditioned way — random initialization without zero-init would have the predictor immediately outputting chaotic values before SIGReg can regularize

Opening
- What should the latent space actually look like? We need a constraint on the embeddings to prevent collapse — but which one, and why? LeJEPA derives the answer from first principles.

Lemmas — Intuition
- The two lemmas both say the same thing from different angles: if some axes in the embedding space have much less spread than others, any task that relies on those weak axes will perform worse. 
- Lemma 1 shows this hurts bias, Lemma 2 shows it hurts variance. 
- Equal spread — isotropy — is the unique covariance structure that is safe for any downstream task.

Theorem 1
- Among all isotropic distributions, the Gaussian is the unique one that also minimizes error for nonlinear probes. 
- So the target is N(0, I) — and collapse is ruled out automatically, since a collapsed distribution is by definition not isotropic.

Opening
- We've established the target.
- Now: how do you enforce N(0, I) during training? 
- Testing a full multivariate Gaussian in high dimensions is quadratic in cost. 
- SIGReg sidesteps this with a sketching trick.

Steps 1 & 2 — Cramer-Wold
- A d-dimensional distribution is fully determined by all its 1D projections. 
- So we project embeddings onto random directions and test each slice for normality. 
- If every slice looks Gaussian, the joint is Gaussian — a hard high-dimensional problem reduced to many cheap univariate tests.

Step 3 — Epps-Pulley
- For each projection, we apply the Epps-Pulley test. Gradients are provably bounded regardless of the input, so training is stable without any clipping or warmup.

Closing
- One loss term, one hyperparameter lambda = 0.1. No stop-gradient, no EMA, no teacher-student network — compared to PLDM's six hyperparameters across seven loss terms.

1. Without Cramér-Wold: Ω(2^d) — exponential, no tractable characterization
2. With Cramér-Wold / full covariance test: O(d²) — quadratic, still expensive at high d (e.g. d = 256 ViT-Tiny patch dim → 65 536 entries)
3. SIGReg sketching: O(d·p) — subquadratic, scales to high-dimensional latents

Setup — non-robotics audience
- Not everyone here may be familiar with model predictive control, so let me start with the core idea
- The world model we just trained is frozen — we never update it again
- At test time: we are given a current frame and a goal frame. The task is to find a sequence of actions that gets us from here to there
- Key insight: instead of trying actions in the real world, we simulate them inside the model — entirely in latent space

Diagram walkthrough
- Both frames are encoded with the same frozen encoder into z_1 (starting latent) and z_g (goal latent)
- We roll out the predictor autoregressively: z_1 + a_1 → ẑ_2 → [P, a_2] → ẑ_3 → … → ẑ_5
- The predictor is deterministic — same latent + same action always gives the same output
- The cost is the squared distance between the final predicted latent ẑ_5 and the goal latent z_g
- CEM then searches for the action sequence (a_1 … a_5) that minimises this cost

PushT callout
- In PushT the action is a 2D relative displacement (Δx, Δy) ∈ [-1, 1]² fed to a PD controller
- Not an absolute position — relative so the same action means the same thing wherever the agent is

CEM walkthrough
- CEM (Cross-Entropy Method) is a zero-order optimizer — it never backpropagates through the world model
- Initialize: 300 complete 5-step action plans sampled from N(0, I)
- Evaluate: roll out all 300 plans in the world model, rank by latent cost
- Keep top 30 elites, refit the Gaussian to their mean and variance
- Sigma shrinks as the distribution concentrates — broad exploration at iter 1, tight cluster at iter 30
- The final mean μ_T is the action plan executed

MPC walkthrough
- MPC (Model Predictive Control) is the outer loop that keeps the plan anchored to the real world
- After CEM converges: execute all H=5 planned steps in the real environment, observe new state, replan
- Like a GPS that recalculates every few seconds rather than committing to the full route upfront
- LeWM uses K=H (execute full plan before replanning) not K=1 (replan every step) — speed vs. reactivity tradeoff

Decoder callout — important distinction
- The predictor rollout frames are decoded using a lightweight transformer decoder — a diagnostic tool only
- This decoder is trained independently and never called during planning or execution
- CEM outputs raw (Δx, Δy) actions applied directly to the environment — no pixel generation in the loop
- This is a key advantage over generative world models (DreamerV3, IRIS): no reconstruction bottleneck at control time

Goal = a real frame from the same offline trajectory, N steps later (always reachable by construction).
Agent gets a step budget to reach that state from a random start. Success = close enough to goal state within budget.

Push-T: T-block must match target pose (position + angle). Budget 50 steps, goal 25 ahead. — LeWM 96% / PLDM 78% / DINO-WM 92%
OGBench-Cube: arm must place cube at 3D target location. Budget 50 steps, goal 25 ahead. — LeWM 74% / DINO-WM 86% (3D visual complexity hurts encoder)
Two-Room: agent (red dot) must cross through door to target in other room. Budget 100 steps, goal 100 ahead. — LeWM 87% / PLDM & DINO-WM ~100% (low intrinsic dim; Gaussian prior over-regularizes)
Reacher: 2-joint arm; success = perfect joint alignment with target config. Budget 50 steps, goal 25 ahead. — LeWM 86% / PLDM 78% / DINO-WM 79%

Note: frame-skip of 5 → each step = 5 raw env timesteps (50 steps = 250 raw timesteps)

Fixed FLOPs (Fig. 3): LeWM 90% vs DINO-WM 13% on PushT — [CLS] token shrinks CEM search space
Speed: 0.98s vs 47s full planning

Linear Probing — freeze encoder, fit linear regressor to predict physics quantities (no labels during training)
R²: agent pos 0.974 / block pos 0.986 / block angle 0.902 — all above PLDM
Linear probe = lower bound: if linear works, the structure is in the representation, not the probe

Violation-of-Expectation — feed physically impossible sequences (teleport, reversed time)
Measure latent prediction error — higher error = more surprised
LeWM reliably assigns higher error to impossible events

Key Findings (left column)
- 2 loss terms: The central claim of the paper. Contrast with PLDM (the only prior end-to-end alternative): 7 loss terms, 6 hyperparameters. LeWM collapses this to prediction loss + SIGReg with a single λ=0.1. No EMA teacher network, no frozen encoder — both were previously considered necessary.
- Zero-shot planning: Emphasize 'same hyperparameters' — not just same architecture, literally the same λ value works across PushT (2D manipulation), OGBench-Cube (3D), TwoRoom (navigation), and Reacher (motion). The model is never shown the task goal during training; planning is purely at inference time.
- Fixed-compute dominance: The [CLS] bottleneck compresses 256 patch tokens down to a single 192-dim vector. At equal FLOP budget: LeWM 90% vs DINO-WM 13% on PushT. Lower-dimensional latent makes CEM's search space more tractable — the speedup and the quality improvement come from the same architectural choice.
- Regularizer shapes planning geometry: This was not designed in — it emerged. The isotropic Gaussian prior happens to produce latent trajectories with cosine similarity ~0.6 (straight paths). PLDM explicitly adds a temporal smoothness loss term and only achieves ~0.4. SIGReg accidentally beats the dedicated mechanism.

Limitations & Future Work (right column)
- CEM doesn't scale to high-DoF: CEM is a zero-order optimizer — it never differentiates through the world model, it just samples random action sequences. For a 7-DoF robot arm, one step is a 7-dim action; H=5 steps is a 35-dim joint search. Sample complexity grows exponentially. The paper mentions this but offers no path forward.
- Not a foundation model: Every new environment requires a full new offline dataset and a full retrain from scratch. DINO-WM sidesteps this by relying on DINOv2 pretrained on 124M images. LeWM has no such transfer — it is environment-specific by construction.
- Hierarchical world models: H=5 is the hard bottleneck for long-horizon tasks. Assembling objects, navigating large rooms — these need 50+ steps. Hierarchical abstraction would allow planning over sub-goals at a coarser time scale.
- Online / interactive learning: The offline assumption means the dataset is fixed before training begins. For real robotics deployment, the model needs to keep learning from new interactions.
